does: This paper critiques and extends vector-grounding accounts of language model semantics by proposing a profile-based theory of reference. Rather than treating reference as a fixed link between expressions and objects, the authors argue reference emerges through context-sensitive, discourse-level patterns shaped by norms and affect. The core contribution is establishing that LLM grounding depends fundamentally on how referential profiles—distributed across finite vector spaces through optimization—are recovered and stabilized through contextual inference and continuation. The paper accepts that vector grounding replaces classical symbol grounding but shows current approaches miss crucial aspects of how reference actually functions, both in human language use and in LLM representations.
connects: This work engages with the recent shift from classical Fregean symbol grounding (which required causal links or intentional states) to vector-space accounts that ground meaning in distributional semantics. It builds on work questioning whether perceptual or embodied grounding is necessary for language understanding and responds to debates about whether transformers can achieve genuine reference. The paper connects to pragmatist philosophy of language emphasizing public stabilization of meaning through use, correction, and norm-governance rather than private mental content. It also extends research on how neural networks parameterize linguistic structure, building on analyses of distributed representations and superposition in vector systems.
enables: This framework opens pathways for understanding how LLMs develop stable referential capacities despite lacking human perception or embodiment. It suggests future work on how to evaluate whether models maintain coherent referential profiles across discourse contexts and how affect-normative dimensions of language might be realized numerically. The profile-based approach could inform methods for detecting and correcting systematic reference failures in LLMs, for understanding cross-lingual grounding differences, and for designing training objectives that better stabilize reference. It also points toward richer accounts of meaning-making in multimodal systems and deeper investigation of how optimization recovers distributed referential patterns from linguistic traces.
